title: Reduce rational expressions to lowest terms: Error analysis
description: Analyze other people's attempts to reduce rational expressions to lowest terms, and identify the mistake they made.
topic_path: Reduce rational expressions to lowest terms: Error analysis

assessment_items.question_content:
Aoi tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2-1}$

This is her work:

>Factoring the numerator: $3x+3=3(x+1)$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2-1=(x+1)(x-1)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 0$ and $x\neq \pm 1$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{3x+3}{x^2-1}&=\dfrac{3\cancel{(x+1)}}{\cancel{(x+1)}(x-1)}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{x-1}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Aoi make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She excluded $x=1$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
She excluded $x=-1$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
She excluded $x=0$, which doesn't make the denominator equal to zero.
She didn't factor the denominator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ryuto tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2+x-6}{x^2-4x+4}$

This is his work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2+x-6=(x+3)(x-2)$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2-4x+4=(x-2)^2$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 0$ and $x\neq 2$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2+x-6}{x^2-4x+4}&=\dfrac{(x+3)\cancel{(x-2)}}{(x-2)\cancel{(x-2)}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x+3}{x-2}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Ryuto make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He didn't exclude $x=-3$, which makes the numerator equal to zero.
He excluded $x=2$, which doesn't make the numerator in the new expression equal to zero.
He excluded $x=0$, which doesn't make the denominator equal to zero.
He didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joyce tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2-2x-8}{2x+4}$

This is her work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2-2x-8=(x+2)(x-4)$

>Factoring the denominator: $2x+4=2(x+2)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 0$ and $x\neq -2$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2-2x-8}{2x+4}&=\dfrac{\cancel{(x+2)}(x-4)}{2\cancel{(x+2)}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x-4}{2}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Joyce make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She didn't exclude $x=4$, which makes the numerator equal to zero.
She excluded $x=-2$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
She excluded $x=0$, which doesn't make the denominator equal to zero.
She didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lindsay tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2-9}{2x^2-6x}$

This is her work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2-9=(x+3)(x-3)$

>Factoring the denominator: $2x^2-6x=2x(x-3)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 0$ and $x\neq \pm 3$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2-9}{2x^2-6x}&=\dfrac{(x+3)\cancel{(x-3)}}{2x\cancel{(x-3)}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x+3}{2x}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Lindsay make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She excluded $x=-3$, which doesn't make the denominator equal to zero.
She excluded $x=3$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
She excluded $x=0$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
She didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Andre tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2+5x+4}{x^2-16}$

This is his work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2+5x+4=(x+1)(x+4)$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2-16=(x+4)(x-4)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq -1$ and $x\neq \pm 4$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2+5x+4}{x^2-16}&=\dfrac{(x+1)\cancel{(x+4)}}{\cancel{(x+4)}(x-4)}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x+1}{x-4}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Andre make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He excluded $x=-1$, which doesn't make the denominator equal to zero.
He excluded $x=-4$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
He excluded $x=4$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
He didn't factor the denominator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Isabel tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{4x^2-4x}{x^2+4x-5}$

This is her work:

>Factoring the numerator: $4x^2-4x=4x(x-1)$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2+4x-5=(x+5)(x-1)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 0$, $x\neq 1$, and $x\neq -5$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{4x^2-4x}{x^2+4x-5}&=\dfrac{4x\cancel{(x-1)}}{(x+5)\cancel{(x-1)}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{4x}{x+5}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Isabel make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She excluded $x=0$, which doesn't make the denominator equal to zero.
She excluded $x=1$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
She excluded $x=-5$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
She didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Raiden tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{4x^2-12x}{3x^2+15x}$

This is his work:

>Factoring the numerator: $4x^2-12x=4x(x-3)$

>Factoring the denominator: $3x^2+15x=3x(x+5)$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{4x^2-12x}{3x^2+15x}&=\dfrac{4\cancel x(x-3)}{3\cancel x(x+5)}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{4(x-3)}{3(x+5)}
\end{align}$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq -5$

**What mistake did Raiden make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He didn't exclude $x=3$, which makes the numerator equal to zero.
He didn't exclude $x=0$, which makes the denominator in the original expression equal to zero.
He excluded $x=-5$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
He didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dennis tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2-6x+9}{x^2+x-12}$

This is his work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2-6x+9=(x-3)^2$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2+x-12=(x-3)(x+4)$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2-6x+9}{x^2+x-12}&=\dfrac{\cancel{(x-3)}(x-3)}{\cancel{(x-3)}(x+4)}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x-3}{x+4}
\end{align}$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq -4$

**What mistake did Dennis make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He excluded $x=-4$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
He didn't exclude $x=3$, which makes the denominator in the original expression equal to zero.
He didn't exclude $x=0$.
He didn't factor the denominator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Makenna tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{5x-20}{x^2-6x+8}$

This is her work:

>Factoring the numerator: $5x-20=5(x-4)$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2-6x+8=(x-4)(x-2)$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{5x-20}{x^2-6x+8}&=\dfrac{5\cancel{(x-4)}}{\cancel{(x-4)}(x-2)}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{x-2}
\end{align}$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 2$

**What mistake did Makenna make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She excluded $x=2$, which doesn't make the numerator equal to zero.
She didn't exclude $x=4$, which makes the denominator in the original expression equal to zero.
She didn't exclude $x=0$.
She didn't factor the denominator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chace tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2-5x+6}{5x-10}$

This is his work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2-5x+6=(x-2)(x+3)$

>Factoring the denominator: $5x-10=5(x-2)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 2$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2-5x+6}{5x-10}&=\dfrac{\cancel{(x-2)}(x+3)}{5\cancel{(x-2)}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x+3}{5}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Chace make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He didn't exclude $x=-3$, which makes the numerator equal to zero.
He excluded $x=2$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
He didn't exclude $x=0$.
He didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Camila tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^2-4x+4}{x^2+4x+4}$

This is her work:

>Factoring the numerator: $x^2-4x+4=(x+2)(x-2)$

>Factoring the denominator: $x^2+4x+4=(x+2)^2$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq -2$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{x^2-4x+4}{x^2+4x+4}&=\dfrac{\cancel{(x+2)}(x-2)}{\cancel{(x+2)}(x+2)}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x-2}{x+2}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Camila make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She didn't exclude $x=2$, which makes the numerator equal to zero.
She excluded $x=-2$, which doesn't make the numerator in the new expression equal to zero.
She didn't exclude $x=0$.
She didn't factor the numerator correctly.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jesse tried to reduce the following expression to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{3x^2-9x}{7x-21}$

This is his work:

>Factoring the numerator: $3x^2-9x=(x+3)(x-3)$

>Factoring the denominator: $7x-21=7(x-3)$

>Finding excluded values: $x\neq 3$

>Reducing to lowest terms:

>$\begin{align}
\dfrac{3x^2-9x}{7x-21}&=\dfrac{(x+3)\cancel{(x-3)}}{7\cancel{(x-3)}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x+3}{7}
\end{align}$

**What mistake did Jesse make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He didn't exclude $x=-3$, which makes the numerator equal to zero.
He excluded $x=3$, which doesn't make the denominator in the new expression equal to zero.
He didn't exclude $x=0$.
He didn't factor the numerator correctly.
